title: Topic 21: Speedrunning and Cheating: Where the Line Gets Blurry
content:
## ABSTRACT

This paper delves into the ethically ambiguous world of video game speedrunning, a subculture where completing a game at breakneck speed is the ultimate prize. We explore the blurry line between "clever use of game mechanics" and "outright cheating," a distinction that has fueled countless forum wars and existential crises among gamers. Through a rigorous, albeit hilarious, analysis of player motivations, psychological drivers, and the philosophical quandaries of glitch exploitation, we seek to understand why some players push the boundaries of fair play. Is it for glory? For efficiency? Or is it simply because they can? By examining high-profile cases of both celebrated and condemned speedruns, this paper sheds light on the complex interplay of competition, community, and the unwritten rules of digital sportsmanship. Our findings suggest that the speedrunner's mindset offers a fascinating microcosm of human behavior, revealing our innate desire to optimize, to win, and occasionally, to redefine the rules of the game entirely.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand pantheon of human achievement, right next to landing on the moon and the invention of the spork, lies the noble art of speedrunning. For the uninitiated, speedrunning is the practice of playing a video game with the singular goal of finishing it as quickly as possible. It is a world where a 100-hour epic role-playing game is brutally condensed into a 20-minute caffeine-fueled fever dream. Speedrunners are the digital equivalent of Olympic athletes, if the Olympics involved exploiting bizarre physics glitches and sequence-breaking a narrative to save a princess in record time. They are pioneers, artists, and, according to some, filthy, rotten cheaters.

This paper wades into the murky, contentious, and often hilarious debate that rages within the speedrunning community: where do we draw the line between legitimate strategy and cheating? Is using a glitch that allows you to clip through a wall and bypass an entire level a stroke of genius or a morally bankrupt shortcut? What about manipulating the game's random number generator to get favorable item drops? Is that just smart planning or a pact with the digital devil? These are the questions that keep speedrunners up at night, fueling heated debates on forums and Discord servers, and occasionally leading to the public shaming of a once-revered champion.

We will explore the psychological underpinnings of this debate, examining the motivations that drive players to dedicate thousands of hours to shaving milliseconds off their personal bests. We will delve into the philosophical arguments that define what constitutes “fair play” in a world where the rules are written in code and are, by their very nature, exploitable. Through a series of case studies, we will examine some of the most infamous and celebrated speedruns in history, from the mind-bending glitches of *The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time* to the frame-perfect precision required in *Super Mario 64*. Our goal is not to provide a definitive answer, but rather to illuminate the complex and often contradictory logic that governs this fascinating subculture. So, grab your popcorn, suspend your disbelief, and prepare to question everything you thought you knew about playing by the rules.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely silent on the subject of cheating in video games. While they may not have been specifically dissecting the frame-by-frame antics of a *Super Metroid* speedrunner, scholars have long been fascinated by the psychological drivers of unfair play. A recurring theme in the literature is the concept of "moral disengagement," a term that sounds like it was lifted from a particularly dry episode of *Star Trek*, but is actually quite relevant. Bandura (1999) posits that individuals can selectively disengage their moral compass, allowing them to act in ways they would normally consider unethical. In the context of gaming, this could manifest as a player convincing themselves that exploiting a glitch is not *really* cheating because it’s part of the game’s code, a digital loophole waiting to be exploited. It’s not a bug; it’s an undocumented feature!

The concept of "flow," as described by Csikszentmihalyi (1990), also offers a compelling lens through which to view speedrunning. Flow is the state of complete immersion in an activity, where the individual is so focused that they lose track of time and self. For speedrunners, achieving a state of flow is the holy grail, allowing them to execute complex sequences of actions with near-perfect precision. However, the pursuit of this state can also lead to a "win-at-all-costs" mentality. If a small, ethically dubious shortcut can help maintain that flow state and lead to a new personal best, the temptation can be overwhelming. As one study on the motivations for cheating in Pokémon GO notes, players will often justify their actions by framing them as a way to enhance their enjoyment of the game, rather than as a way to gain an unfair advantage (Paay, Kjeldskov, & Internicola, 2018).

Of course, no discussion of cheating would be complete without mentioning the role of the community. The speedrunning community, like any other, has its own set of norms and values. These are not written in stone, but are constantly being negotiated and renegotiated. What is considered a legitimate strategy in one game might be seen as a cardinal sin in another. The use of emulators, for example, is a particularly contentious issue. While some argue that emulators allow for a more level playing field by eliminating hardware variations, others see them as a gateway to a world of save states and other digital abominations. The community’s reaction to a particular speedrun is often the final arbiter of its legitimacy. A run that is celebrated by the community is, for all intents and purposes, a legitimate run, regardless of how many walls were clipped through or bosses were skipped. As one Reddit user on r/speedrun sagely noted, “If the community says it’s legit, it’s legit. End of story.”

## ANALYSIS

The speedrunner’s psyche is a fascinating cocktail of perfectionism, obsession, and a flexible relationship with the concept of rules. At the heart of the “is it cheating?” debate lies the psychological phenomenon of **cognitive dissonance**. A player may hold two conflicting beliefs: “I am a highly skilled player who has mastered this game,” and “I just skipped half the game by jumping backwards through a wall.” To resolve this mental discomfort, the player must redefine their definition of “cheating.” The glitch is no longer a cheat; it is a “strat,” a “skip,” or a “clever use of game mechanics.” It’s a sophisticated mental gymnastics routine that would make a yogi jealous.

This re-contextualization is often aided by the community’s creation of specific categories. The proliferation of categories like “Any%” (finish the game as fast as possible, no holds barred), “Glitchless” (no major glitches allowed), and “100%” (collect every item, which often has its own set of allowed glitches) is a testament to the community’s attempt to manage this dissonance on a macro scale. By creating a sandbox for every type of playstyle, the community effectively says, “It’s not cheating if you’re honest about what you’re doing.” This is a form of self-regulation that is both pragmatic and, in its own way, deeply philosophical. It acknowledges that there is no one “right” way to play a game, but rather a spectrum of approaches, each with its own set of unwritten rules.

Then there is the allure of the forbidden, the thrill of the exploit. For some, the line between speedrunning and hacking is a blurry one. **Tool-Assisted Speedruns (TAS)** are a perfect example of this. In a TAS, the player uses an emulator and other tools to create a theoretically perfect run, one that is impossible for a human to perform. Every input is optimized, every frame is perfect. While TAS runs are not considered legitimate in the same way as human-performed runs, they are celebrated for their artistry and for what they reveal about the game’s hidden potential. They are a form of sanctioned cheating, a way for the community to explore the outer limits of a game’s code without sullying the purity of human competition.

Ultimately, the speedrunner’s mindset is one of extreme optimization. They are constantly looking for ways to shave milliseconds off their time, to find a more efficient route, to exploit a previously unknown quirk in the game’s physics. In this context, a glitch is not a moral failing; it is a resource. It is a tool to be used, a puzzle to be solved. The real sin, in the eyes of the speedrunning community, is not using a glitch, but lying about it. The community values transparency and honesty above all else. As long as you are upfront about the methods you used, you will be judged not on the purity of your run, but on the skill and ingenuity with which you executed it.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

The annals of speedrunning are filled with tales of triumph, heartbreak, and good old-fashioned drama. These are not just stories about video games; they are parables about human nature, ambition, and the eternal struggle between doing things the “right” way and doing them the fast way. Let’s take a look at a few of the most illustrative examples.

**The Curious Case of the Impossible Coin:** For years, a single, solitary coin in the “Tiny-Huge Island” level of *Super Mario 64* was considered impossible to collect. It was a tantalizing prize, a digital white whale that taunted speedrunners and completionists alike. The coin was located in such a way that it seemed impossible for Mario to reach it without the help of a glitch that was, at the time, unknown. Then, in 2014, a Tool-Assisted Speedrun (TAS) was created that showed it was, in fact, possible. The TAS used a series of frame-perfect inputs to manipulate Mario’s momentum in a way that no human could ever hope to replicate. The video of the TAS was a revelation, a glimpse into a world of possibilities that had previously been considered science fiction. It was a moment of pure, unadulterated joy for the community, a celebration of the beauty of the exploit. It also served as a stark reminder of the vast gulf that separates human and machine, and the ever-blurring line between what is possible and what is “fair.”

**The *Minecraft* Speedrunner Who Flew Too Close to the Sun:** In the world of *Minecraft* speedrunning, where random chance plays a significant role, the temptation to tip the scales in one’s favor can be overwhelming. Such was the case with one popular speedrunner who was caught red-handed after a meticulous investigation by the community. The speedrunner’s luck was just a little too good, their item drops a little too perfect. The community, armed with a deep understanding of the game’s code and a healthy dose of skepticism, went to work. They analyzed hours of footage, crunched the numbers, and eventually proved, beyond a shadow of a doubt, that the speedrunner had been manipulating the game’s files to get favorable results. The fallout was swift and brutal. The speedrunner was stripped of their records, their reputation was shattered, and they became a cautionary tale for anyone who would dare to put their thumb on the digital scales. The incident was a powerful demonstration of the community’s ability to self-police and its unwavering commitment to the integrity of the sport.

**The Great “Glitchless” Debate:** The *Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time* speedrunning community is a hotbed of philosophical debate. At the center of it all is the question of what, exactly, constitutes a “glitch.” Is it a glitch to use a bomb to propel yourself across a gap that you’re not supposed to be able to cross? Is it a glitch to use a specific sequence of inputs to skip a cutscene? The community is divided. Some argue that any exploit that was not intentionally programmed by the developers is a glitch and should be banned from “glitchless” runs. Others argue that if it’s in the game, it’s fair game. The debate has been raging for years, and there is no end in sight. It is a perfect example of the subjective and ever-evolving nature of the rules in speedrunning. What is considered a legitimate strategy today might be seen as a dirty trick tomorrow. The only thing that is certain is that the debate will continue, and that is, in its own way, part of the fun.

## DISCUSSION

The world of speedrunning, with its byzantine rules and passionate debates, may seem like a niche and esoteric subculture. But to dismiss it as such would be to miss the forest for the trees. The behaviors and motivations on display in the speedrunning community offer a fascinating window into the broader landscape of human psychology and consumer behavior. At its core, speedrunning is a form of conspicuous consumption, but instead of flaunting wealth, players flaunt their mastery of a particular skill. The “capital” in this economy is not money, but time – both the time invested in mastering the game and the time shaved off the world record.

This obsession with efficiency and optimization is a hallmark of modern consumer culture. We are constantly bombarded with messages about how to save time, be more productive, and get the most out of our purchases. The speedrunner is the ultimate embodiment of this mindset, a hyper-consumer who has taken the logic of the marketplace and applied it to the world of play. They are not just playing a game; they are optimizing a system, and in doing so, they are reflecting a broader cultural shift towards the gamification of everyday life.

The debate over what constitutes “cheating” in speedrunning also has a parallel in the world of consumerism. Is it “cheating” to use a coupon to get a discount on a product? Is it “cheating” to use a loophole in a company’s return policy to get a refund on a product you’ve already used? The answer, as in speedrunning, is not always clear. It depends on your personal code of ethics, the unwritten rules of the marketplace, and the extent to which you are willing to bend the rules to get what you want. The speedrunner’s willingness to exploit glitches and loopholes is not a sign of moral decay, but rather a reflection of a deeply ingrained human desire to find the path of least resistance.

Finally, the speedrunning community’s emphasis on transparency and community validation offers a powerful counter-narrative to the often-isolating nature of modern consumerism. In a world where we are increasingly encouraged to see ourselves as individual consumers, the speedrunning community reminds us of the importance of shared values and collective judgment. The community, not the individual, is the final arbiter of what is and is not acceptable. This is a powerful lesson, and one that has implications that extend far beyond the world of video games.

## CONCLUSION

In the final analysis, the line between speedrunning and cheating is not a line at all, but a blurry, shifting, and endlessly fascinating frontier. It is a space where the rules are constantly being rewritten, where ingenuity is prized above all else, and where the only real sin is getting caught. The speedrunner, in their relentless pursuit of perfection, holds up a mirror to our own society, reflecting our obsession with efficiency, our flexible relationship with the rules, and our deep-seated desire to win. Whether they are seen as digital athletes or glorified cheaters, one thing is certain: they are pushing the boundaries of what is possible, one frame-perfect, wall-clipping, boss-skipping run at a time. And in a world that is increasingly defined by its digital landscapes, that is a pursuit that is both entertaining and, dare we say, important. . . important. The next time you see a player contorting a game into an unrecognizable pretzel of glitches and exploits, don't be so quick to judge. You might just be witnessing the birth of a new art form.

## REFERENCES

Bandura, A. (1999). Moral disengagement in the perpetration of inhumanities. *Personality and Social Psychology Review*, *3*(3), 193-209.

Csikszentmihalyi, M. (1990). *Flow: The psychology of optimal experience*. Harper & Row.

Paay, J., Kjeldskov, J., & Internicola, D. (2018). Motivations and practices for cheating in Pokémon GO. *Proceedings of the 20th International Conference on Human-Computer Interaction with Mobile Devices and Services*, 1-12.
